stem: Petra Donnison listed personal qualities mental health nurses must possess. A student selects an option claiming to list ALL of them precisely. Which is MOST correct without addition or omission?
options: Patient; concerned for and able to build relationships with others; mature and responsible; able to work well under pressure; able to deal with people from a variety of backgrounds and cultures
Patient; compassionate; concerned for others; mature and responsible; able to work under pressure; empathetic; able to deal with diverse backgrounds
Patient; able to build relationships with others; mature; responsible; able to work under pressure; able to deal with people from diverse cultures; having good communication skills
Patient; concerned for others; mature and responsible; able to work well under pressure; able to communicate with people from diverse backgrounds

stem: Guthrie (2008) identified key qualities for nurses working with patients who need someone for a period of time. Which description is MOST precisely stated from the notes?
options: The key qualities are to be caring, compassionate, and have the desire to reach out to someone who, for a period of time, is in need
The key qualities are to be caring, compassionate, and have the willingness to reach out to someone who, for a period of time, is experiencing distress
The key qualities are to be caring, empathetic, and have the desire to reach out to someone who, for a period of time, requires professional support
The key qualities are to be caring, compassionate, and have the commitment to reach out to someone who, for a period of time, cannot help themselves

stem: CASE STUDY: A student nurse is asked to recall the personal qualities of the psychiatric mental health nurse according to Burger (2010). She must identify the COMPLETE and EXACT set of traits. Which is MOST accurate?
options: Good communication skills, empathy, and a genuine attitude of caring; confidence, patience, and a sense of humour are also helpful when working with patients who have severe mental disorders
Good communication skills, empathy, and a genuine attitude of caring; confidence, patience, and a sense of compassion are also helpful when working with patients who have severe mental disorders
Good communication skills, empathy, and a genuine attitude of caring; warmth, patience, and a sense of humour are also helpful when working with patients who have severe mental disorders
Good communication skills, empathy, and a genuine attitude of caring; confidence, tolerance, and a sense of humour are also helpful when working with patients who have severe mental disorders

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q3): After the session, a student argues that 'a sense of humour' seems unprofessional for psychiatric nursing. Which response MOST accurately reflects the context in the notes?
options: A sense of humour is specifically described as helpful when working with patients who have severe mental disorders â it is a recognised personal quality alongside confidence and patience, not an unprofessional trait
A sense of humour is listed as an optional trait that is only appropriate when working with mild mental health conditions, not with patients who have severe mental disorders
A sense of humour is described as important for nursing team cohesion and staff wellbeing but is not specifically mentioned as relevant to direct patient care in psychiatric settings
A sense of humour is mentioned as potentially harmful in psychiatric settings because patients with severe disorders may misinterpret humour as mockery, so it requires careful professional discretion

stem: Aroha Webby described physical requirements for mental health nurses. Which statement is MOST precisely and completely stated from the notes?
options: Mental health nurses need to be reasonably fit and healthy to cope with the stress and pressure of the job; they may also have to spend long periods on their feet; Burger (2010) added that it can be stressful to be surrounded by people suffering from mental illness on a regular basis; psychiatric nurses must be willing to get help for themselves if they start becoming depressed or anxious as a result of their job or other situations in life; all nurses need to manage time effectively and work hard

Mental health nurses need to be physically fit and healthy to manage the demands of shift work; they must maintain good nutrition and rest; Burger (2010) stated that psychiatric work can be emotionally exhausting; nurses must seek help if they develop burnout; all nurses need to maintain professional boundaries and manage their workload effectively

Mental health nurses need to be reasonably fit and healthy to cope with stress; they may have to stand for long periods; Burger (2010) stated that it can be distressing to work alongside severely ill patients; psychiatric nurses must recognise signs of vicarious trauma and seek clinical supervision; all nurses need to practise self-care consistently

Mental health nurses need to be physically capable to manage potentially aggressive patients; they must maintain fitness for manual handling; Burger (2010) noted that working in psychiatry can cause secondary traumatisation; nurses must use staff support services when affected; all nurses need to balance workload and self-care effectively

stem: The functions of the psychiatric nurse can be organised into categories. Which set of categories is MOST accurately identified from the notes?
options: Physical and biological interventions; psychiatric medication; electroconvulsive therapy; physical care; psychosocial interventions; spiritual interventions

Physical and biological interventions; psychiatric medication; electroconvulsive therapy; physical care; psychosocial interventions; spiritual interventions; observation; listening; empathy; note taking
Physical care; psychiatric medication; electroconvulsive therapy; psychological interventions; spiritual care; observation and assessment
Biological interventions; medication management; electroconvulsive therapy; physical rehabilitation; psychosocial therapy; spiritual assessment

stem: CASE STUDY: A ward nurse explains the nurse's role in electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). Which statement is MOST accurately grounded in the notes?
options: Psychiatric mental health nurses are involved in the administration of ECT and assist with preparation and recovery from the treatment, which involves anaesthesia; it is used in only a tiny proportion of cases and only after all other possible treatments have been exhausted; approximately 85% of clients receiving ECT have major depression, with the remainder having schizoaffective disorder, mania, or schizophrenia

Psychiatric mental health nurses administer ECT independently in most cases and monitor patients for post-ictal confusion and cardiovascular complications; ECT is used in approximately 50% of treatment-resistant cases and is indicated primarily for schizophrenia and severe depression

Psychiatric mental health nurses assist with preparation for ECT but are not involved in recovery as anaesthesiology teams take over post-procedure; ECT is used in approximately 85% of patients with severe depression and requires informed consent exclusively from the next of kin

Psychiatric mental health nurses are involved in ECT preparation and post-procedure care; ECT is used as a first-line treatment for major depression after pharmacological augmentation fails; the procedure does not involve anaesthesia in modern practice due to advances in unilateral electrode placement

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q7): A student asks which mental disorders are listed as primary indications for ECT. The MOST accurate answer from the notes is:
options: Approximately 85% have major depression as the indication for use, with the remainder having schizoaffective disorder, mania, or schizophrenia
Approximately 85% have major depression as the indication for use, with the remainder having bipolar disorder, mania, or schizophrenia
Approximately 85% have schizoaffective disorder as the indication for use, with the remainder having major depression, mania, or schizophrenia
Approximately 85% have treatment-resistant depression as the indication for use, with the remainder having schizoaffective disorder, bipolar disorder, or psychosis

stem: Psychosocial interventions are described in the notes. Which statement MOST precisely captures all elements without adding or substituting terms?
options: Psychosocial interventions are increasingly delivered by nurses in mental health settings and include psychotherapy interventions such as cognitive behavioural therapy, family therapy, and less commonly other interventions such as milieu therapy or psychodynamic approaches; they can be applied to psychosis, depression, and anxiety; nurses work with people over a period of time and use psychological methods to teach psychological techniques for recovery and future crisis management

Psychosocial interventions are primarily delivered by clinical psychologists but increasingly delegated to nurses; they include cognitive behavioural therapy, dialectical behaviour therapy, and psychodynamic approaches; they are applied to depression, anxiety, and personality disorders; nurses use evidence-based methods to teach coping strategies

Psychosocial interventions are delivered exclusively by nurses with advanced practice qualifications; they include family therapy, group therapy, and cognitive behavioural therapy; they are indicated for psychosis and mood disorders; nurses use structured therapeutic programmes to build recovery skills

Psychosocial interventions are increasingly delivered by nurses in mental health settings and include cognitive behavioural therapy, family therapy, interpersonal therapy, and acceptance and commitment therapy; they are applied across all diagnostic categories; nurses teach psychological techniques to aid recovery

stem: The basis of spiritual interventions is described in the notes. Which statement MOST accurately reflects their foundation and content?
options: Spiritual interventions look at mental illness from the perspective of a spiritual crisis; they focus on developing a sense of meaning, purpose, and hope; they involve listening to the person's story and facilitating connection to God, a greater power, or greater whole â possibly through meditation or prayer; this may be religious or non-religious depending on the individual's spirituality

Spiritual interventions look at mental illness from the perspective of religious deficit; they focus on reintegrating the patient into their faith community; they involve scripture reading, religious counselling, and prayer with the patient; they are only applicable in religiously observant patients

Spiritual interventions look at mental illness from the perspective of existential suffering; they focus on meaning-making through philosophical inquiry; they involve guided imagery, mindfulness meditation, and narrative therapy; they are delivered exclusively by chaplains in collaboration with the nursing team

Spiritual interventions look at mental illness from the perspective of a moral crisis; they focus on restoring ethical congruence and social identity; they involve community reintegration and forgiveness work; they are particularly relevant for patients with guilt-based depression or religious obsessional disorder

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse explains why spiritual interventions are particularly important in the Nigerian context. Which statement MOST accurately reflects the notes?
options: Spiritual interventions are particularly important in Africa in general and Nigeria in particular because people interpret mental illness so much from a spiritual point of view
Spiritual interventions are particularly important in Nigeria because traditional healers are still the primary healthcare providers for psychiatric disorders in most communities
Spiritual interventions are particularly important in Nigeria because the healthcare system lacks sufficient psychiatric medication, making spiritual approaches the most accessible treatment option
Spiritual interventions are particularly important in Nigeria because cultural stigma prevents patients from engaging with conventional psychiatric treatment, necessitating a spiritual entry point

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q11): The nurse also explains that spiritual interventions emphasise a specific concept. Which concept is MOST precisely stated in the notes?
options: The importance of engagement
The importance of therapeutic alliance
The importance of hope and meaning
The importance of spiritual assessment

stem: The purpose of the assessment stage in the nursing process is identified in the notes. Which statement is MOST precise?
options: The purpose of the assessment stage is to identify the patient's nursing problems, which are expressed as either actual or potential
The purpose of the assessment stage is to diagnose the patient's psychiatric condition using standardised rating scales and clinical interview techniques
The purpose of the assessment stage is to identify the patient's medical and nursing problems and formulate a care plan addressing both dimensions
The purpose of the assessment stage is to collect baseline data on the patient's physical and psychological status for comparison with future assessments

stem: CASE STUDY: A nursing student is asked to define 'signs' versus 'symptoms' in nursing assessment. Which distinction is MOST precisely stated in the notes?
options: Signs are things that can be observed or measured; symptoms are things that can be felt by the patient, such as nausea or vertigo
Signs are subjective patient reports such as pain; symptoms are objective findings that the nurse observes on examination
Signs are findings identified by the physician on physical examination; symptoms are findings identified by the nurse during psychosocial assessment
Signs are objective findings documented in the nursing record; symptoms are subjective findings communicated by the patient during the therapeutic interview

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q14): The student is then asked which assessment techniques are mentioned in the notes for psychiatric nursing. Which list is MOST complete and accurate?
options: Inspection, palpation, auscultation, and percussion in addition to the vital signs of temperature, blood pressure, pulse, and respiratory rate; further examination of body systems such as cardiovascular or musculoskeletal
Inspection, palpation, and auscultation of vital signs including temperature, blood pressure, pulse, and respiratory rate; mental state examination and neurological assessment
Inspection, palpation, percussion, and auscultation of vital signs including temperature, blood pressure, and pulse rate; psychological testing and behavioural observation
Inspection, palpation, and auscultation only; vital signs are taken by healthcare assistants, not nurses; the nurse focuses on psychosocial assessment in psychiatric settings

stem: Peplau (1952) described the role of observation in the therapeutic interaction. Which statement is MOST precisely stated from the notes?
options: Peplau used observation as the first step in the therapeutic interaction; the nurse observes the client's behaviour and guides him or her in giving detailed descriptions of behaviour

Peplau used observation as the second step in the therapeutic interaction following rapport establishment; the nurse observes verbal and non-verbal behaviour and reflects observations back to the patient
Peplau used observation as the first step in the therapeutic interaction; the nurse observes both verbal and non-verbal cues and uses these to formulate the nursing diagnosis
Peplau used observation as a continuous process throughout the therapeutic interaction; the nurse observes behaviour at every stage and uses these observations to adjust the therapeutic approach

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is observing a patient with schizophrenia during a therapeutic interaction. Which type of observation is specifically emphasised in the notes for psychiatric mental health nursing?
options: Observing the speaker's nonverbal actions as he or she communicates or behaves
Observing the speaker's verbal content exclusively for signs of thought disorder, loosening of associations, and tangentiality
Observing the patient's vital signs and physical presentation for signs of medication side effects during the therapeutic encounter
Observing the patient's interaction patterns with other patients and staff to assess social functioning and relational capacity

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q17): The nurse asks her preceptor what determines whether she is truly 'actively listening.' Which elements are specifically listed in the notes as essential for active listening in psychiatric nursing?
options: The nurse must not only hear the patient but also be attending and attentive; interpersonal and communication techniques such as summarising, clarifying, reflecting, and providing eye contact are essential to understand and relate to the patient

The nurse must maintain eye contact, avoid interrupting, use open-ended questions, and reflect the patient's emotional tone back; active listening also requires the nurse to suppress personal reactions to patient disclosures

The nurse must hear, paraphrase, and reflect the patient's words; avoid closed-ended questions; and maintain a calm therapeutic posture; active listening is demonstrated primarily through verbal techniques rather than non-verbal attending

The nurse must be fully present, maintain therapeutic neutrality, avoid all personal disclosure, and use silence strategically; active listening requires the deliberate suppression of the nurse's own internal dialogue

stem: Listening is defined in the notes with a precise description. Which is the MOST accurate and complete statement?
options: Listening involves refraining from other internal mind activities and concentrating exclusively on what the client is saying; while listening to a client's story it is almost impossible for the nurse not to make assumptions; a person's life experiences, knowledge base, values, and prejudices often colour the interpretation of a message

Listening involves refraining from internal distractions and concentrating on what the client is saying; active listening requires the nurse to suspend all personal judgements; a person's cultural background and emotional state often colour the interpretation of a message

Listening involves refraining from other internal mind activities and focusing exclusively on the client's verbal content; while listening to a client it is entirely possible for a skilled nurse to remain free from assumptions; a person's trauma history and attachment style often colour the interpretation of a message

Listening involves refraining from other mental activities and concentrating on what the client communicates; active listening is a learned skill that eliminates the tendency to make assumptions; a person's cognitive style and communication preferences often colour the interpretation of a message

stem: In therapeutic communication, what does the nurse MOST specifically do to address the challenge of assumptions during listening?
options: Ask numerous specific questions to get the real story from the client's perspective, to help answer questions and clarify assumptions, and to develop empathy with the client
Use reflective listening techniques to mirror the client's exact words and thereby confirm understanding without imposing the nurse's own interpretations
Maintain therapeutic silence to allow the client to fill interpretive gaps independently, reducing the risk of the nurse's assumptions distorting the communication
Document the client's words verbatim and review them with a supervisor to identify where the nurse's assumptions may have influenced the interpretation of the message

stem: CASE STUDY: A charge nurse tells a student: 'Active listening is considered to be one of the core elements of something.' What is that 'something'?
options: Understanding
Therapeutic communication
Empathy
The therapeutic relationship

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q21): The student then asks what specific research finding about active listening is mentioned in the notes. Which statement is MOST accurately stated?
options: When psychiatric/mental health nurses gave proper attention and appeared interested, patients felt understood
When psychiatric/mental health nurses gave proper attention and appeared interested, patients felt respected and disclosed more personal information
When psychiatric/mental health nurses gave proper attention and appeared interested, patients experienced reduced anxiety and improved therapeutic engagement
When psychiatric/mental health nurses gave proper attention and appeared interested, patients achieved better medication adherence and symptom management outcomes

stem: Empathy is described as a complex concept with specific components. Which statement MOST precisely identifies ALL three components as listed?
options: Empathy is a complex multi-dimensional concept that has moral, cognitive, emotive, and behavioural components
Empathy is a complex multi-dimensional concept that has cognitive, affective, and behavioural components
Empathy is a complex multi-dimensional concept that has moral, emotional, and interpersonal components
Empathy is a complex multi-dimensional concept that has cognitive, emotive, and relational components

stem: Clinical empathy involves three specific abilities, listed as (a), (b), and (c) in the notes. Which is the MOST accurate and complete statement?
options: (a) Understand the patient's situation, perspective, and feelings and their attached meanings; (b) communicate that understanding and check its accuracy; (c) act on that understanding with the patient in a helpful (therapeutic) way

(a) Understand the patient's situation, perspective, and emotions; (b) reflect that understanding back to the patient verbally; (c) act on that understanding by adapting the nursing care plan accordingly

(a) Understand the patient's situation, perspective, and feelings; (b) communicate that understanding to the multidisciplinary team; (c) act on that understanding by implementing evidence-based therapeutic interventions

(a) Understand the patient's situation, perspective, and feelings and their attached meanings; (b) validate that understanding by asking the patient to confirm its accuracy; (c) act on that understanding by adjusting medication and care based on the patient's expressed needs

stem: CASE STUDY: A mental health nursing lecturer asks students about the research status of empathy. Which statement from the notes is MOST accurate?
options: Research on the effect of empathy on health outcomes in primary care is lacking, but studies in mental health and in nursing suggest it plays a key role; empathy can be improved and successfully taught at schools especially if it is embedded in the students' actual experiences with patients

Research on the effect of empathy on health outcomes in primary care is extensive and consistently demonstrates that empathic nursing reduces hospital readmission rates; empathy is primarily a natural trait that cannot be meaningfully taught

Research on the effect of empathy on health outcomes in primary care is lacking, but studies in psychiatry confirm that empathic nursing significantly reduces psychotic relapse; empathy cannot be taught but can be cultivated through reflective practice

Research on the effect of empathy on health outcomes in primary care is mixed, with some studies showing benefit and others showing no effect; studies in nursing suggest empathy is beneficial but difficult to standardise across diverse patient populations

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q25): A student asks which assessment tools have been used to evaluate empathy. Which statement from the notes is MOST accurate?
options: A variety of assessment and feedback techniques have also been used in general medicine, psychiatry, and nursing (Mercer & Reynolds, 2002)
The Jefferson Scale of Empathy has been validated across multiple clinical specialties and is the primary tool referenced for assessing empathy in psychiatric nursing practice
The Toronto Empathy Questionnaire and Interpersonal Reactivity Index are specifically cited as the validated tools used to assess empathy in psychiatric nursing students
Standardised patient encounters and video-recorded therapeutic interactions are cited as the gold-standard assessment methods for measuring empathy in nursing practice

stem: The ethical code governing psychiatrists was first set forth in a specific document and year. Which statement is MOST precisely stated in the notes?
options: The psychiatric code of ethics was first set forth through the Declaration of Hawaii in 1977, expanded through a 1983 Vienna update, and in 1996 through the broader Madrid Declaration; the code was further revised in Hamburg in 1999

The psychiatric code of ethics was first set forth through the Declaration of Helsinki in 1977, expanded through a 1983 Vienna update, and in 1996 through the broader Madrid Declaration; the code was further revised in Geneva in 1999

The psychiatric code of ethics was first set forth through the Declaration of Hawaii in 1977, expanded through a 1986 Vienna update, and in 1996 through the broader Madrid Declaration; the code was further revised in Hamburg in 2002

The psychiatric code of ethics was first set forth through the Declaration of Hawaii in 1977, expanded through a 1983 Vienna update, and in 1999 through the broader Madrid Declaration; the code was further revised in Hamburg in 2003

stem: CASE STUDY: A student lists the topics covered by the World Psychiatric Association ethical code. Which list is MOST accurately and completely derived from the notes?
options: Patient assessment, up-to-date knowledge, human dignity of incapacitated patients, confidentiality, research ethics, sex selection, euthanasia, organ transplantation, torture, media relations, genetics, and ethnic or cultural discrimination

Patient assessment, informed consent, human dignity of incapacitated patients, confidentiality, research ethics, sex selection, euthanasia, organ transplantation, torture, media relations, and genetic discrimination

Patient assessment, up-to-date knowledge, human dignity of incapacitated patients, confidentiality, research ethics, sex selection, euthanasia, organ transplantation, the death penalty, media relations, genetics, and ethnic or cultural discrimination

Patient assessment, up-to-date knowledge, human dignity of incapacitated patients, confidentiality, research ethics, sex selection, euthanasia, organ transplantation, the death penalty, torture, media relations, genetics, and ethnic or cultural discrimination

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q28): A student asks who first described these ethical elements in the notes. The MOST accurate attribution is:
options: The World Psychiatric Association (WPA, 2011) issued the ethical code governing psychiatrists
The American Nurses Association issued the code of ethics for psychiatric nursing and it was attributed to Gluzman (1991) and Debreu (1988)
Christina Ash, an eHow Contributor, described the code of ethics governing psychiatric nurses in the U.S.
Queens University of North Carolina identified the ethical principles that guide the activities and duties of psychiatric/mental health nurses

stem: The ethical element of RESPECT in psychiatric nursing is described in the notes. Which statement MOST precisely reflects ALL its elements?
options: In all professional relationships the nurse has respect for the inherent worth, diversity, and right of choice of all patients; upholds the elements of informed consent; recognises the uniqueness of individuals; and provides care regardless of the socioeconomic status of the client or the nature of his health problems

In all professional relationships the nurse has respect for the inherent worth and dignity of all patients; upholds the principle of autonomy; recognises the individuality of each patient; and provides care regardless of the patient's diagnosis or personal background

In all professional relationships the nurse has respect for the inherent worth, diversity, and right of self-determination of all patients; upholds the elements of confidentiality; recognises the uniqueness of individuals; and provides care regardless of the socioeconomic status or religious affiliation of the client

In all professional relationships the nurse has respect for the inherent worth, diversity, and right of choice of all patients; upholds the elements of informed consent; recognises the uniqueness of each patient; and provides care regardless of the socioeconomic status or cultural background of the client

stem: CASE STUDY: A psychiatric nurse is reviewing the ethical element of 'Health, Mental Health and Well-Being' from the notes. Which statement is MOST accurately stated?
options: The nurse respects the physiological, psychological, and developmental needs of each individual; understands the interconnections of mental health and physical health and that they are influenced by lifestyle and perceptions; and has an obligation to preserve his own health, mental health, and well-being

The nurse respects the physical, psychological, and social needs of each individual; understands that mental health and physical health are separate domains requiring different professional approaches; and has an obligation to preserve his own physical health and wellbeing

The nurse respects the biopsychosocial needs of each individual; understands that mental health affects physical health but not the reverse; and has an obligation to seek clinical supervision to preserve his own mental health and wellbeing

The nurse respects the physiological, psychological, and spiritual needs of each individual; understands the interconnections of mental health and physical health and that they are influenced by culture and society; and has an obligation to preserve his own health through regular supervision

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q31): The nurse then reviews 'Privacy and Confidentiality.' What is stated about the nurse's obligation?
options: Each psychiatric nurse has an obligation to protect the health, rights, and safety of the patient; the physical privacy of the patient and the confidentiality of any information he discloses are parts of this issue

Each psychiatric nurse has an obligation to protect the health, rights, and dignity of the patient; the physical privacy of the patient and the confidentiality of medical records are parts of this issue

Each psychiatric nurse has an obligation to protect the safety, rights, and autonomy of the patient; the emotional privacy of the patient and the confidentiality of any information he discloses are parts of this issue

Each psychiatric nurse has an obligation to protect the health, rights, and safety of the patient; the physical privacy of the patient, the confidentiality of information, and the security of patient data are parts of this issue

stem: Queens University of North Carolina identified five principles that guide psychiatric/mental health nurses. Which list is MOST precisely and completely stated?
options: The right of individuals to decide their own destiny; the duty of a nurse to promote the welfare of others; the obligation to avoid harming others; treating all patients equally and fairly; speaking the truth

The right of individuals to decide their own destiny; the duty of a nurse to promote the welfare of others; the obligation to avoid harming others; treating all patients with dignity and fairness; speaking the truth honestly

The right of individuals to self-determination; the duty of a nurse to promote the welfare of others; the obligation to avoid harming others; treating all patients equally and fairly; maintaining confidentiality

The right of individuals to decide their own destiny; the duty of a nurse to promote the welfare of others; the obligation to do no harm; treating all patients equally and fairly; speaking the truth in all professional interactions

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse faces a patient who wants to commit suicide. The notes present this scenario. Which ethical challenge is MOST precisely identified?
options: The right of individuals to decide their own destiny collides with the belief that suicide is not a rational choice
The nurse's duty to protect life collides with the patient's right to refuse treatment in terminal cases
The obligation to avoid harming others collides with the patient's autonomous decision to end their own suffering
The principle of truth-telling collides with the therapeutic need to maintain hope in suicidal patients

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q34): The notes present specific examples of suicide scenarios. Which examples are MOST precisely listed?
options: A terminal cancer patient in tremendous agony; a 33-year-old woman with no job, money, or home
A terminal cancer patient in tremendous agony; a 45-year-old man with treatment-resistant depression and no social support
A patient in the final stages of a degenerative neurological illness; a 33-year-old woman with severe financial hardship
A terminal cancer patient with uncontrolled pain; a young adult with chronic homelessness and psychiatric comorbidity

stem: What is the psychiatric profession's position on reparative therapy for homosexuality, according to the notes?
options: The Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nursing says the psychiatric profession considers such treatments harmful to the patients and for nurses to participate, even with someone who wants the treatment, would be a serious breach of ethics

The American Psychiatric Association states that reparative therapy is ineffective but not harmful; participation by nurses is discouraged but not considered an ethical breach when the patient provides informed consent

The World Psychiatric Association considers reparative therapy experimental and permits its use only when the patient provides voluntary informed consent and the treatment is conducted by a licensed psychiatrist

The Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nursing says the psychiatric profession considers such treatments unproven; nurses may not initiate reparative therapy but may support patients who seek it from other providers

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is discussing patient rights with a student. Queens University states patients have specific rights regarding refusing treatment. Which statement is MOST accurately and completely stated from the notes?
options: Patients have the right to refuse medication; they also have the right to choose the least restrictive alternative treatment â for example, outpatient treatment rather than being institutionalised; this is a right even if a nurse believes more restrictive alternatives would work better

Patients have the right to refuse medication; they also have the right to choose the least invasive treatment â for example, psychotherapy rather than medication; this is a right even if the psychiatrist believes pharmacological treatment would be more effective

Patients have the right to refuse all treatment; they also have the right to choose community-based over institutional care; this right can be overridden only by a court order when the patient lacks capacity

Patients have the right to refuse medication; they also have the right to choose the least restrictive alternative treatment; this right is suspended during involuntary admission when the patient is deemed a danger to self or others

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q37): The student asks: 'Is the patient's right to least restrictive treatment absolute even against the nurse's clinical judgement?' The MOST accurate answer from the notes is:
options: Yes â this is a right even if a nurse believes more restrictive alternatives would work better
No â the nurse's clinical judgement takes precedence when the patient lacks insight or poses a risk to themselves or others
Partially â the patient's preference is considered but the final decision rests with the multidisciplinary team based on evidence-based guidelines
No â the patient's right to least restrictive treatment applies only in outpatient settings; in inpatient settings the nurse's clinical judgement takes precedence

stem: Psychosocial interventions are described as based on a specific practice model. Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: Psychosocial interventions are based on evidence-based practice and therefore the techniques tend to follow set guidelines based on what has been demonstrated to be effective by nursing research

Psychosocial interventions are based on humanistic theory and therefore the techniques are individually tailored without adherence to set guidelines, emphasising the therapeutic relationship over structured protocols

Psychosocial interventions are based on cognitive theory and therefore the techniques target maladaptive thought patterns using structured cognitive protocols validated by randomised controlled trials

Psychosocial interventions are based on behavioural theory and therefore the techniques involve systematic desensitisation, exposure therapy, and operant conditioning applied within the nurse-patient relationship

stem: CASE STUDY: A student challenges the nurse: 'You said psychosocial interventions are evidence-based, but hasn't that been criticised?' The nurse's MOST accurate response from the notes is:
options: There has been some criticism that evidence-based practice is focused primarily on quantitative research and should reflect also a more qualitative research approach that seeks to understand the meaning of people's experience
There has been some criticism that evidence-based practice excludes cultural factors and is therefore not applicable across all patient populations, particularly in non-Western psychiatric settings
There has been some criticism that evidence-based practice in psychiatry relies too heavily on pharmaceutical industry-funded research and therefore systematically overestimates medication efficacy
There has been some criticism that evidence-based practice creates rigid protocols that prevent nurses from exercising clinical judgement and responding to the uniqueness of individual patient needs

stem: Which of the following MOST accurately describes the NHfo of UK Courses and Careers Department's view on personal qualities for mental health nursing?
options: To work with people who experience mental health problems requires an interest in working with people, good listening and communication skills, a compassionate nature, and a willingness to help and develop in people the skills to live their lives
To work with people who experience mental health problems requires empathy, strong professional boundaries, clinical knowledge, resilience, and the ability to de-escalate challenging behaviour

To work with people who experience mental health problems requires good listening skills, evidence-based clinical practice competencies, reflective practice skills, and ability to work effectively in multidisciplinary teams

To work with people who experience mental health problems requires compassion, cultural competence, advanced communication skills, self-awareness, and proficiency in psychosocial assessment techniques

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse explains to a student why 'helping someone to regain such skills and to live independently' is specifically described as rewarding in the notes. What specific skills does the text use as examples?
options: Skills others take for granted such as getting on a bus, doing the shopping, or cooking a meal
Skills others take for granted such as managing finances, using public transport, or maintaining a household
Skills others take for granted such as personal hygiene, preparing food, and managing medication independently
Skills others take for granted such as building social relationships, maintaining employment, and engaging in recreational activities

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q42): The student asks what sense of satisfaction specifically results from helping patients with personal care. Which statement is MOST accurately derived from the notes?
options: Helping people with their personal care can similarly offer job satisfaction in knowing that you have contributed towards maintaining their quality of life by helping the patient to feel comfortable and cared for
Helping people with their personal care offers professional fulfilment in knowing that you have restored their functional independence and reduced their reliance on nursing staff
Helping people with their personal care offers personal satisfaction in knowing that you have formed a genuine therapeutic connection and supported the patient's sense of dignity
Helping people with their personal care offers clinical satisfaction in knowing that you have addressed a fundamental nursing need and contributed to the patient's physical recovery

stem: Psychiatric nurses can prescribe medication according to the notes. Which statement most accurately qualifies this?
options: Nurse practitioners can prescribe medication; nurses will monitor for side effects and response to medical treatments by using assessments
All registered psychiatric nurses can prescribe medication; nurses will monitor for side effects and response to medical treatments using standardised assessment tools
Nurse practitioners can prescribe medication only under direct physician supervision; nurses will monitor for side effects using nursing observation checklists
Advanced practice psychiatric nurses and nurse practitioners can prescribe medication; basic registered nurses monitor for side effects using physiological assessment only

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks about the nurse's role in medication education. Which statement is MOST precisely grounded in the notes?
options: Nurses will also offer information on medication so that, where possible, the person in care can make an informed choice, using the best evidence available
Nurses will also provide detailed pharmacological information on medication to ensure the patient understands the mechanism of action and potential side effects before consenting to treatment
Nurses will also counsel patients on medication to promote adherence and reduce the risk of self-discontinuation in community mental health settings
Nurses will also educate patients on medication schedules and administration techniques so that patients can eventually self-manage their treatment under minimal supervision

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q45): The student asks about the role of the nurse in psychiatric medication overall. The MOST complete answer from the notes is:
options: Many psychiatric mental health nurses are involved in the administration of medicines, both in oral (e.g., tablet or liquid) form or by intramuscular injection; nurse practitioners can prescribe medication; nurses will monitor for side effects and response to medical treatments by using assessments; nurses will also offer information on medication for informed choice using the best evidence available

Many psychiatric mental health nurses are involved in the administration of medicines in oral and intramuscular form; advanced practice nurses prescribe medication; nurses document medication responses and report adverse effects; nurses provide written medication education materials to patients

Many psychiatric mental health nurses administer oral and parenteral medications; all psychiatric nurses can prescribe; nurses assess medication response using structured rating scales; nurses ensure patients sign consent forms before each medication administration

Many psychiatric mental health nurses administer oral, intramuscular, and intravenous medications; nurse practitioners prescribe under physician supervision; nurses monitor therapeutic drug levels through blood sampling; nurses provide patient education on medication interactions

stem: The American Nurses Association's Code of Ethics for psychiatric nurses is described in the notes. Which element about its scope is MOST accurately stated?
options: The code sets out guidelines for acceptable behaviour for all nurses in the U.S.; however, as the therapeutic relationship between the psychiatric nurse and the client is key to successful psychiatric/mental health care, there are some elements of the code that are more strongly applicable for these nurses and not only in the U.S. but also applicable all over the world

The code sets out guidelines for acceptable behaviour exclusively for psychiatric nurses in the U.S. and has been formally adopted by nursing associations in 47 other countries as the international standard
The code sets out guidelines for acceptable behaviour for all nurses in the U.S. and is supplemented by the WPA ethical code for psychiatric practice in international settings

The code sets out guidelines for acceptable behaviour for psychiatric nurses in the U.S. only; its application in other countries is not recommended because mental health legislation varies significantly across jurisdictions

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is evaluating psychosocial interventions she delivered. Which assessment approach is MOST explicitly stated in the notes as the basis for these interventions?
options: A nursing assessment that includes observation or measurement of signs and symptoms, using a framework based on a nursing model
A standardised psychiatric rating scale assessment conducted at admission and reviewed weekly to track therapeutic progress
A biopsychosocial assessment formulation completed by the multidisciplinary team during the initial care planning meeting
A functional assessment of activities of daily living and social functioning conducted by the occupational therapist in collaboration with the nurse

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q48): A colleague challenges the nurse: 'Assessment is the first stage of the nursing process â what makes it holistic in psychiatry?' The MOST accurate response from the notes is:
options: Assessment is the first stage of the nursing process in which the nurse carries out a complete and holistic nursing assessment of every patient's needs, regardless of the reason for the encounter; usually an assessment framework based on a nursing model is used

Assessment is the first stage of the nursing process in which the nurse carries out a standardised biopsychosocial evaluation of the patient's functional status, symptom burden, and social support networks

Assessment is the first stage of the nursing process in which the nurse collects subjective and objective data from all available sources including the patient, family, records, and other healthcare professionals

Assessment is the first stage of the nursing process in which the nurse uses validated screening tools, clinical interviews, and behavioural observation to identify psychiatric diagnosis and nursing care priorities

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks why physical care is a function of the psychiatric nurse. The MOST accurate explanation from the notes is:
options: Along with other nurses, psychiatric mental health nurses will intervene in areas of physical care to ensure that patients have good levels of personal hygiene, nutrition, and sleep, and attend to any concomitant physical ailments

Along with other nurses, psychiatric mental health nurses will intervene in physical care to ensure that patients' basic needs are met and to prevent the complications of prolonged psychiatric hospitalisation

Along with other nurses, psychiatric mental health nurses will intervene in physical care to prevent medical complications secondary to psychiatric medications such as metabolic syndrome and cardiovascular disease

Along with other nurses, psychiatric mental health nurses will intervene in physical care because psychiatric patients frequently neglect self-care as a symptom of their mental illness, requiring nursing assistance to maintain basic hygiene

stem: According to Petra Donnison, one personal quality of mental health nurses is that they must be able to deal with people from a variety of _______________ and _______________.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'Burger (2010) said that good communication skills, empathy, and a genuine attitude of caring are very important traits; _______________ and a sense of humour are also helpful when working with patients who have severe mental disorders.' Two traits are missing.
ECT treatment is used in only a tiny proportion of cases and only after all other possible treatments have been _______________.
CASE STUDY: An exam reads: 'Approximately _______________% of clients receiving ECT have major depression as the indication for use.' The precise percentage from the notes must be supplied.
Active listening is considered to be one of the core elements of _______________ in psychiatric nursing.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'Listening involves refraining from other internal mind activities and concentrating _______________ on what the client is saying.' One precise adverb from the notes must be supplied.

Peplau (1952) used _______________ as the first step in the therapeutic interaction, during which the nurse observes the client's behaviour and guides him or her in giving detailed descriptions of behaviour.

CASE STUDY: A nurse educator asks: 'In psychiatric mental health nursing, observation involves observing the speaker's _______________ actions as he or she communicates or behaves.' Only one word fills this blank precisely.
Empathy is described as a complex _______________ concept that has moral, cognitive, emotive, and behavioural components.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'Clinical empathy involves the ability to (b) communicate that understanding and _______________ its accuracy.' One precise verb from the notes must be supplied.
The psychiatric code of ethics was first set forth through the Declaration of _______________ in 1977.
CASE STUDY: A student is quizzed: 'The World Psychiatric Association code was further revised in _______________ in 1999.' Only one correct city completes this sentence.
Queens University of North Carolina lists five principles; the second is: 'The duty of a nurse to promote the _______________ of others.'
CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'According to the notes, treatments to turn homosexuals straight are known as _______________ therapy.' The precise clinical term from the notes must be supplied.
Patients have the right to choose the least _______________ alternative treatment, even if the nurse believes more restrictive alternatives would work better.

CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'Spiritual interventions focus on developing a sense of meaning, _______________, and hope for the person in their current life experience.' One precise noun from the notes must be supplied.
Psychosocial interventions can be applied to a broad range of problems including psychosis, _______________, and anxiety.

CASE STUDY: A nurse exam reads: 'There has been some criticism that evidence-based practice is focused primarily on _______________ research and should reflect also a more qualitative research approach.' One precise adjective from the notes must be supplied.

In therapeutic communication, the nurse must ask numerous specific questions to get the real story from the client's perspective, to help answer questions and clarify _______________, and to develop empathy with the client.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'According to the broader Madrid Declaration, the WPA ethical code covers ethnic or cultural _______________.' Only one precise noun from the notes completes this sentence.
